title: Severe Hypothyroidism Before Urgent Oral-Cancer Surgery
subtitle: TSH >100, IV LT4 unavailable — oral loading vs 150 mcg, when fT4 (not TSH) clears the OT, reduce to ~100 mcg now, and the adrenal screen you must not skip

relatedChapters.title: Post-Thyroidectomy Levothyroxine Start in a Patient with Stable CAD
Decompensated Hypothyroidism After Swapping Levothyroxine for an Herbal Supplement

patient.chiefComplaint: Pre-operative fitness assessment for urgent oncological surgery (oral cancer)
pmh: Severe untreated primary hypothyroidism — not on levothyroxine at first visit

caseDescription:
A 60-year-old man is scheduled for **urgent oncological surgery** for oral cancer and attends for fitness assessment.

**10 June 2026 (first visit):** Incidental severe overt primary hypothyroidism discovered — TSH >100 mIU/L, total T4 4.27 (low). He was not on thyroid replacement. Intravenous levothyroxine was **not available**; he is a poor patient without access to parenteral formulations. In view of achieving rapid fitness for surgery, oral levothyroxine **150 mcg/day** was started (essentially full replacement).

**24 June 2026 (~14 days later):** Repeat thyroid function — TSH 1.67 mIU/L, T4 12.4, T3 1.67 — all normal. He is now biochemically euthyroid and considered fit for surgery.

**Resident decision point:** Continue 150 mcg? Reduce dose? What would published literature have recommended at initiation — especially where IV LT4 is unavailable?
keyFindings: Severe overt hypothyroidism at presentation: TSH >100, low T4 4.27 — not on replacement
Urgent oral-cancer surgery; IV levothyroxine unavailable (resource-limited setting)
Oral LT4 150 mcg/day for ~2 weeks → TSH 1.67, T4 12.4, T3 1.67 (biochemically euthyroid)
Best option now: **reduce to ~100 mcg/day** (≈1.6 mcg/kg) and proceed with surgery
Ideal initial approach (literature): IV loading 200–500 mcg ± empiric hydrocortisone, or oral loading 300–500 mcg day 1 → ~1.6 mcg/kg maintenance; target **normal fT4**, not TSH, before OT
Critical teaching gap: screen for adrenal insufficiency (morning cortisol) before starting LT4 when TSH >100
finalDiagnosis: Severe untreated primary hypothyroidism rendered biochemically euthyroid on oral levothyroxine — reduce maintenance dose to weight-based replacement (~100 mcg/day) and proceed with urgent oncological surgery
learningObjectives: Stratify perioperative hypothyroidism severity and decide when urgent surgery can proceed
Choose the correct levothyroxine dose after rapid biochemical normalisation — avoid iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis in a 60-year-old
Apply IV and oral loading protocols when severe hypothyroidism must be corrected quickly and IV LT4 is unavailable
Recognise the pre-operative biochemical endpoint (normal fT4, not TSH) and the mandatory adrenal-insufficiency screen before LT4

items.title: Case snapshot: severe hypothyroidism discovered during urgent onco-surgery work-up
items.content:
60-year-old man, urgent oral-cancer surgery planned.
10/06/26: TSH >100, T4 4.27 (low), not on levothyroxine. IV LT4 unavailable.
Started oral LT4 150 mcg/day to achieve rapid fitness.
24/06/26 (~14 days): TSH 1.67, T4 12.4, T3 1.67 — all normal.
He is biochemically fit; the question is maintenance dose and whether the initial strategy matched best evidence.
keyPoints: Severe overt hypothyroidism (TSH >100, low T4) found at pre-op assessment.
Oral 150 mcg/day × ~2 weeks → rapid biochemical normalisation.
Decision now: dose reduction vs continue 150 mcg vs other.
Retrospective teaching: what should initiation have looked like?
items.reference: Case vignette (user-provided): "TSH >100 not on rx, T4 4.27 low — started 150 mcg/day in view of achieving rapid fitness" and "TSH 1.67, T4 12.4, T3 1.67 all normal" after ~2 weeks.

items.title: Why reduce to ~100 mcg/day now — not continue 150 mcg
items.content: With TSH 1.67 and T4 12.4 (mid-normal) after only ~2 weeks on 150 mcg, continuing full replacement risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis — especially dangerous at age 60 perioperatively (atrial fibrillation, ischaemia). Weight-based maintenance for a typical 65–70 kg adult is ~100–112 mcg/day (1.6 mcg/kg), not 150 mcg. The TSH fall from >100 to 1.67 in two weeks is faster than the usual 4–6-week pituitary re-equilibration, suggesting the system is being pushed hard. He has biochemical headroom: reduce to ~100 mcg, give the morning dose on the day of surgery with a sip of water, and recheck TFT at 4–6 weeks post-op. Stopping LT4 entirely would relapse hypothyroidism within 1–2 weeks (LT4 half-life ~6–7 days).
keyPoints: TSH 1.67 + T4 12.4 on 150 mcg → over-replacement risk if dose held.
Maintenance ≈1.6 mcg/kg (~100–112 mcg for ~65–70 kg), not 150 mcg.
Reduce to ~100 mcg; proceed with surgery; recheck TFT post-op.
Do not stop LT4 — relapse within 1–2 weeks.
items.reference: Weight-based dosing (ATA hypothyroidism guidance, Jonklaas et al., Thyroid 2014): "The full replacement dose of levothyroxine in adults is approximately 1.6 mcg/kg body weight per day" — applied to this 60-year-old after rapid normalisation.

items.title: Why the pre-operative endpoint is normal fT4 — not normal TSH
items.content: TSH is a lagging indicator: after starting levothyroxine, serum T4 rises within days, but pituitary TSH suppression can take 4–6 weeks to fully equilibrate. For urgent surgery, the relevant pre-operative biochemical target is restoration of circulating thyroid hormone — normal free T4 (and clinical improvement) — not waiting for TSH to normalise. In this case, T4 12.4 and T3 1.67 are already normal at 2 weeks while TSH has paradoxically normalised quickly; either way, the patient is fit for OT based on hormone levels. Chasing a 'perfect' TSH before urgent cancer surgery would delay oncological care without adding safety once fT4/T4 is restored.
keyPoints: T4/fT4 normalises in days; TSH lags weeks.
Pre-op target: normal fT4 + clinical fitness, not necessarily normal TSH.
This patient: T4/T3 normal at 2 weeks → cleared for surgery.
Do not delay urgent onco surgery waiting for TSH equilibration.
items.reference: Perioperative hypothyroidism review (Malhotra & Bhadada, Indian J Endocr Metab 2022): "In urgent surgeries or emergencies, surgery is acceptable with untreated hypothyroidism — then under strict monitoring and possibly with parenteral thyroxine administration" — with the corollary that once hormone levels are restored, delay is unnecessary.

items.title: Why adrenal function must be assessed before levothyroxine in severe primary hypothyroidism
items.content: This is the single most important missed step in the case. In severe primary hypothyroidism (TSH >100), coexisting autoimmune adrenal insufficiency is not rare — a prospective study found significantly lower morning cortisol in patients with TSH >100, with ~8.5% meeting adrenal-insufficiency criteria. Physiologically, hypothyroidism lowers cortisol clearance and requirement; initiating levothyroxine increases cortisol metabolism and can unmask or precipitate acute adrenal crisis if the adrenals cannot mount a response. AACE/ATA guidance (Grade II, B) states glucocorticoids should precede levothyroxine when combined insufficiency is suspected. At minimum: morning serum cortisol before starting LT4; if low or if empiric cover is chosen in a severely ill patient, hydrocortisone 50–100 mg IV q8h until adrenal insufficiency is excluded — especially before a loading dose of LT4.
keyPoints: TSH >100: check morning cortisol before LT4 — ~8.5% may have AI.
LT4 increases cortisol clearance → can precipitate adrenal crisis.
If concerned: hydrocortisone before or with LT4 loading.
This step was missed here — key resident teaching point.
items.reference: AACE/ATA hypothyroidism guidelines (Garber et al., Endocr Pract 2012): "Treatment with glucocorticoids in patients with combined adrenal insufficiency and hypothyroidism should precede treatment with L-thyroxine."

items.title: How to stratify perioperative hypothyroidism severity and surgical timing
items.content: Mild: subclinical (normal fT4, elevated TSH) — urgent surgery generally safe with minor complication risk. Moderate: overt hypothyroidism (elevated TSH, low fT4) without severe clinical features — urgent surgery may proceed with awareness of higher intraoperative hypotension and GI/neuropsychiatric complications. Severe: very low T4/fT4, altered mentation, pericardial effusion, heart failure, or myxedema coma — highest perioperative risk; treat aggressively before or during surgery with close monitoring. This patient (TSH >100, T4 4.27, no myxedema coma) is moderate-to-severe overt disease — significant enough to warrant prompt replacement, but not myxedema coma. Because the indication is urgent oncological surgery (not elective), proceeding once biochemically replete is appropriate; elective cases would ideally wait until effectively treated.
keyPoints: Stratify: subclinical → mild → moderate overt → severe/myxedema.
This case: moderate-to-severe overt, not myxedema coma.
Urgent onco surgery: treat promptly, proceed when fT4 normal.
Elective surgery: delay until effectively treated.
items.reference: Malhotra & Bhadada, Indian J Endocr Metab 2022: "Mild and moderate hypothyroid patients may undergo urgent surgery without delay, with the knowledge that minor perioperative complications might develop" and "Severe hypothyroidism includes patients with severe clinical symptoms such as altered mentation, pericardial effusion, or heart failure, those with very low T4 (<1.0 µg/dL) or fT4 (<0.5 ng/dL), or myxedema coma."

items.title: How IV levothyroxine loading would have been the gold-standard initial approach
items.content: When severe hypothyroidism must be corrected urgently and IV formulation is available, guidelines endorse immediate IV levothyroxine: a loading dose of 200–500 mcg IV, then 50–100 mcg IV daily (or oral equivalent ~100 mcg) until surgery. IV bioavailability is ~100% versus ~70–80% oral. If myxedema or hemodynamic instability: add IV liothyronine (T3) 5–20 mcg load, then 2.5–10 mcg q8h for faster symptomatic relief (T3 acts in hours; T4 in days). Empiric IV glucocorticoid (hydrocortisone) should accompany loading in severe disease until adrenal insufficiency is excluded. Target: normal fT4 by day 5–7, then transition to oral maintenance at ~1.6 mcg/kg (≈100–112 mcg for this patient).
keyPoints: IV load 200–500 mcg → 50–100 mcg/day IV or oral equivalent.
Add IV T3 if myxedema/instability; add empiric hydrocortisone.
Target normal fT4 by day 5–7 for urgent OT clearance.
Maintenance thereafter ≈1.6 mcg/kg oral (~100–112 mcg).
items.reference: Malhotra & Bhadada, Indian J Endocr Metab 2022: "If urgent or emergent surgery is required, hormone replacement therapy should be initiated immediately with IV levothyroxine, using a loading dose of 200 to 500 µg followed by 50 to 100 µg daily."

items.title: How oral levothyroxine loading replaces IV when parenteral formulation is unavailable
items.content: In India and many centres, IV levothyroxine is not stocked — oral loading is the practical alternative endorsed by emerging evidence. Regimens: oral loading 300–500 mcg on day 1, then taper over 3–5 days to high-dose maintenance, OR a single oral load of 500 mcg followed by ~1.6 mcg/kg/day. Case series (including PGIMER Chandigarh and Colombian cohorts) show normalisation of free T4 in all patients by day 4–5 with oral loading in myxedema coma. For this patient — severe but not comatose — an evidence-refined pathway would be: (1) morning cortisol ± empiric hydrocortisone; (2) oral LT4 loading 300–500 mcg day 1; (3) maintenance 100–112 mcg/day from day 2; (4) recheck fT4 at day 5–7; (5) proceed to OT once fT4 normal and clinically improved. Starting 150 mcg/day without a loading dose worked here but overshot maintenance; the loading approach achieves faster fT4 restoration with a lower steady maintenance dose.
keyPoints: IV unavailable: oral load 300–500 mcg day 1 → ~1.6 mcg/kg maintenance.
fT4 normalises by day 4–5 in published oral-loading series.
Screen/cover adrenal insufficiency before loading.
150 mcg/day without load worked but likely exceeds long-term need.
items.reference: Malhotra & Bhadada, Indian J Endocr Metab 2022: "If IV levothyroxine is not available, then oral LT4 regimen consisting of a loading dose of 300–500 µg, followed by taper" and ETJ 2021 (PGIMER): "High-dose oral levothyroxine was effective in achieving early biochemical recovery in patients with myxedema coma."

items.title: How to manage levothyroxine on the day of surgery and during post-operative NPO
items.content: On the day of surgery: administer the usual replacement dose orally with a sip of water on the morning of surgery — no need to convert to IV or double the dose. If the patient is NPO post-operatively for a few days, levothyroxine can safely be omitted briefly; its half-life is approximately 6–7 days in euthyroid patients, so short interruptions do not cause acute decompensation. Resume the usual oral dose as soon as enteral intake is possible. Avoid calcium, iron, and PPIs within ~4 hours of LT4. After surgery, recheck TSH and free T4 at 4–6 weeks (allowing steady state after the dose reduction to 100 mcg) and titrate to TSH 0.5–2.5 mIU/L for long-term maintenance.
keyPoints: Day of surgery: usual PO dose with sip of water — no IV conversion.
Post-op NPO 2–3 days: safe to omit (long half-life).
Resume PO dose when eating; separate from calcium/iron/PPI.
Recheck TFT 4–6 weeks post-op; target TSH 0.5–2.5.
items.reference: Palace et al., Health Serv Insights 2017: "The usual replacement dose can be administered on the day of surgery via the enteral route without the need for any additional supplementation or conversion to a parenteral route of administration" and "If oral medications cannot be given post-operatively thyroid hormones can be safely omitted for a few days."

items.title: Why proceeding with surgery is appropriate now
items.content: The patient has moved from severe overt hypothyroidism (TSH >100, low T4) to biochemical euthyroidism in ~2 weeks. Retrospective studies show hypothyroid surgical patients have higher rates of intraoperative hypotension (non-cardiac surgery) and heart failure (cardiac surgery), plus GI and neuropsychiatric complications — risks that are mitigated by restoration of thyroid hormone. With T4 12.4 and T3 1.67 normal, the hormone deficit has been corrected. Delaying urgent oral-cancer surgery further would prioritise thyroid kinetics over oncological urgency without incremental safety benefit. The remaining task is dose optimisation (reduce to ~100 mcg) to avoid overshoot, not postponement of surgery.
keyPoints: Biochemically euthyroid → perioperative hypothyroid risk substantially reduced.
Urgent onco indication: do not delay for TSH 'perfection'.
Optimise dose (reduce to ~100 mcg), then operate.
Untreated severe hypothyroidism was the risk — that has been addressed.
items.reference: Malhotra & Bhadada, Indian J Endocr Metab 2022: "In severe thyroid disease where the surgical risk is the highest, a multimodal approach with medical and airway management, along with close hemodynamic monitoring, is necessary for those who must undergo urgent surgery."

items.stem: A 60-year-old man with urgent oral-cancer surgery planned was started on levothyroxine 150 mcg/day for severe hypothyroidism (TSH >100, low T4). After 14 days: TSH 1.67, T4 12.4, T3 1.67 — all normal. He is clinically well. What is the best levothyroxine plan now?
options: Continue 150 mcg/day and proceed to surgery
Stop levothyroxine and recheck TFT in 6 weeks before surgery
Reduce to ~100 mcg/day and proceed to surgery
Increase to 200 mcg/day to suppress TSH further before surgery
items.explanation: TSH 1.67 with T4 12.4 on 150 mcg suggests adequate — likely excessive — replacement. Weight-based maintenance is ~1.6 mcg/kg (~100–112 mcg for a typical 65–70 kg man), not 150 mcg. Reducing to ~100 mcg maintains euthyroidism while avoiding iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis (AF, ischaemia risk at age 60). Stopping LT4 causes relapse; continuing 150 mcg risks overshoot; 200 mcg would be dangerous.
items.reference: Case vignette + weight-based dosing: "TSH 1.67, T4 12.4, T3 1.67 all normal" on 150 mcg; maintenance "1.6 µg/kg (~100–112 µg)" for ~65–70 kg adult.

items.stem: For urgent surgery in a patient being rapidly repleted with levothyroxine for severe hypothyroidism, which biochemical endpoint best indicates readiness for the operating theatre?
options: TSH <2.5 mIU/L on two consecutive samples 4 weeks apart
Normal free T4 (with clinical improvement), even if TSH has not fully equilibrated
T3 above the upper limit of normal to confirm conversion
TSH suppression below 0.1 mIU/L to maximise metabolic rate
items.explanation: TSH lags weeks behind serum T4. For urgent surgery, restoring circulating thyroid hormone (normal fT4/T4) and clinical improvement is the operative target — not waiting for full TSH equilibration, which would delay oncological care unnecessarily.
items.reference: Malhotra & Bhadada, IJEM 2022: urgent surgery pathway once hormone replacement initiated — "target normal fT4" before OT; TSH equilibration takes 4–6 weeks.

items.stem: IV levothyroxine is unavailable. A 60-year-old man with TSH >100 and low T4 needs urgent oral-cancer surgery. Which initial oral levothyroxine strategy best matches published evidence for rapid correction?
options: Start 25 mcg/day and titrate every 6 weeks ('start low, go slow')
Oral loading dose 300–500 mcg day 1, then maintenance ~1.6 mcg/kg/day; check fT4 at day 5–7
Withhold LT4 until after surgery to avoid cardiovascular stress
Start 150 mcg/day without loading and wait 6 weeks for TSH normalisation
items.explanation: 'Start low, go slow' is for stable elderly/cardiac outpatients, not urgent severe hypothyroidism needing rapid fT4 restoration. Oral loading (300–500 mcg day 1 → ~1.6 mcg/kg maintenance) achieves normal fT4 by day 4–5 in published series. Withholding LT4 leaves severe disease untreated; 150 mcg without loading may work but delays pool restoration and tends to overshoot maintenance.
items.reference: Malhotra & Bhadada, IJEM 2022: "oral LT4 regimen consisting of a loading dose of 300–500 µg, followed by taper" when IV unavailable; ETJ 2021: "early biochemical recovery" by day 4–5 with oral loading.

items.stem: Before starting levothyroxine in a patient with TSH >100 mIU/L and low T4, what is the most important additional step supported by guidelines?
options: Routine thyroid ultrasound before any LT4
Morning serum cortisol to screen for coexisting adrenal insufficiency
Empiric beta-blocker to prevent thyrotoxicosis from LT4
Wait for anti-TPO antibodies before starting replacement
items.explanation: Severe primary hypothyroidism (TSH >100) carries ~8.5% risk of coexisting adrenal insufficiency. LT4 increases cortisol clearance and can precipitate adrenal crisis. Morning cortisol is a standard, cost-effective screen; empiric hydrocortisone may accompany loading doses in severe disease until AI is excluded.
items.reference: AACE/ATA 2012: "Treatment with glucocorticoids in patients with combined adrenal insufficiency and hypothyroidism should precede treatment with L-thyroxine." Lazare et al. (2025): lower cortisol in TSH >100 cohort.

items.stem: On the morning of surgery, a patient on stable levothyroxine 100 mcg/day for recently corrected hypothyroidism is nil by mouth for the operation. What should be done with levothyroxine?
options: Convert to IV levothyroxine 500 mcg immediately before induction
Double the dose the night before and omit on surgery day
Give the usual 100 mcg orally with a sip of water on the morning of surgery
Withhold all thyroid hormone until post-op day 7
items.explanation: The usual replacement dose is given orally on the morning of surgery with a sip of water — no IV conversion or dose escalation needed. Short post-op NPO periods are safe because LT4's long half-life (~6–7 days) provides a buffer.
items.reference: Palace et al., Health Serv Insights 2017: "The usual replacement dose can be administered on the day of surgery via the enteral route without the need for any additional supplementation or conversion to a parenteral route of administration."

items.stem: A patient with overt hypothyroidism (elevated TSH, low fT4) without myxedema coma requires urgent oncological surgery. When should surgery proceed?
options: Only after TSH is <2.5 on two samples 6 weeks apart
Immediately without any thyroid replacement — urgency overrides all risk
After levothyroxine repletion with normalised fT4 and clinical improvement, without waiting for full TSH equilibration
Only after IV liothyronine has been given for at least 4 weeks
items.explanation: Mild-to-moderate overt hypothyroidism: urgent surgery may proceed once thyroid hormone is restored (normal fT4, clinical improvement). Delaying for full TSH normalisation is unnecessary in oncological urgency. Immediate surgery without any replacement carries higher perioperative risk; routine 4-week IV T3 is not required absent myxedema coma.
items.reference: Malhotra & Bhadada, IJEM 2022: "Mild and moderate hypothyroid patients may undergo urgent surgery without delay" and treat severe disease promptly with monitoring for those requiring urgent OT.

items.statement: Before urgent surgery in a patient being started on levothyroxine for severe hypothyroidism, TSH must be normalised to <2.5 mIU/L on two consecutive samples before proceeding to the operating theatre.
items.explanation: False. TSH lags weeks behind T4. The operative target is normal fT4/T4 and clinical improvement — not full TSH equilibration, which would unnecessarily delay urgent oncological surgery.
items.reference: Malhotra & Bhadada, IJEM 2022: "The relevant pre-operative biochemical target is restoration of circulating thyroid hormone — normal free T4" — TSH equilibration takes 4–6 weeks.

items.statement: When IV levothyroxine is unavailable, high-dose oral levothyroxine loading (300–500 mcg) followed by maintenance therapy is supported by published evidence for rapid biochemical correction.
items.explanation: True. Multiple case series (PGIMER, Colombian cohort, IJEM 2022 review) support oral loading when IV is unavailable, with fT4 normalisation observed by day 4–5.
items.reference: Malhotra & Bhadada, IJEM 2022: "oral LT4 regimen consisting of a loading dose of 300–500 µg, followed by taper" when IV unavailable.

items.statement: Levothyroxine can safely be omitted for a few days post-operatively if the patient is NPO, without acute risk of hypothyroid decompensation.
items.explanation: True. Levothyroxine has a half-life of approximately 6–7 days; brief post-operative interruptions while NPO do not cause acute decompensation in a recently repleted patient.
items.reference: Palace et al., Health Serv Insights 2017: "If oral medications cannot be given post-operatively thyroid hormones can be safely omitted for a few days."

items.statement: Starting levothyroxine in severe primary hypothyroidism without assessing adrenal function is safe because hypothyroidism itself raises cortisol levels.
items.explanation: False. Severe hypothyroidism lowers cortisol production and clearance; LT4 initiation increases cortisol metabolism and can precipitate adrenal crisis if coexisting adrenal insufficiency is present (~8.5% in TSH >100 cohorts).
items.reference: AACE/ATA 2012: "Treatment with glucocorticoids in patients with combined adrenal insufficiency and hypothyroidism should precede treatment with L-thyroxine." Lazare et al. (2025): "significantly lower serum cortisol levels" in TSH >100 patients.

items.statement: In this case, continuing levothyroxine 150 mcg/day after TSH has fallen to 1.67 mIU/L with normal T4 is the safest maintenance strategy for a 60-year-old perioperative patient.
items.explanation: False. 150 mcg likely exceeds weight-based maintenance (~100–112 mcg at 1.6 mcg/kg). Continuing it risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis — arrhythmia and ischaemia — in a 60-year-old perioperatively.
items.reference: Case vignette: "TSH 1.67, T4 12.4" on 150 mcg; ATA guidance: "full replacement dose of levothyroxine in adults is approximately 1.6 mcg/kg body weight per day" (~100–112 µg).

items.assertion: This patient should have his levothyroxine reduced to approximately 100 mcg/day before urgent oral-cancer surgery.
items.reason: Because TSH 1.67 with T4 12.4 on 150 mcg/day suggests he is at or above weight-based maintenance, and continuing 150 mcg risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis in a 60-year-old.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion: biochemical normalisation on a supra-maintenance dose warrants reduction to ~1.6 mcg/kg (~100 mcg) to avoid perioperative thyrotoxic complications while maintaining euthyroidism.
items.reference: Case vignette: "TSH 1.67, T4 12.4" on 150 mcg; maintenance "1.6 µg/kg (~100–112 µg)."

items.assertion: Morning serum cortisol should be checked before initiating levothyroxine in a patient with TSH >100 mIU/L.
items.reason: Because levothyroxine increases cortisol clearance and can precipitate acute adrenal crisis if coexisting adrenal insufficiency is present.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion: the metabolic interaction between thyroid and adrenal axes makes screening (and empiric cover when indicated) essential before LT4, especially with loading doses.
items.reference: AACE/ATA 2012 (Garber et al.): "Treatment with glucocorticoids in patients with combined adrenal insufficiency and hypothyroidism should precede treatment with L-thyroxine" — LT4 increases cortisol clearance.

items.assertion: This patient can proceed to urgent surgery now without waiting for a 6-week TSH recheck.
items.reason: Because T4 and T3 are already normal at 2 weeks, indicating circulating thyroid hormone has been restored.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion: normal T4/T3 confirms hormone restoration — the operative biochemical target — so delaying for TSH equilibration is unnecessary in urgent oncological surgery.
items.reference: Case vignette: "T4 12.4, T3 1.67 all normal" at 2 weeks; Malhotra & Bhadada: urgent surgery once treated/monitored.

items.question: Why might an oral levothyroxine loading dose (300–500 mcg day 1) followed by ~112 mcg/day maintenance achieve faster surgical fitness than starting 150 mcg/day without loading?
items.answer: Severe hypothyroidism depletes the total body T4 pool; a loading dose rapidly replenishes circulating and tissue stores, normalising fT4 within days (published series: day 4–5). Maintenance at ~1.6 mcg/kg (~100–112 mcg for this patient) then sustains euthyroidism without overshoot. Starting 150 mcg/day without loading still works but relies on gradual pool accumulation, takes longer to reach steady hormone levels, and — as this case shows — may exceed long-term maintenance needs once the pituitary TSH responds.
keyPoints: Loading replenishes depleted T4 pool → fT4 normal in days.
Maintenance ~1.6 mcg/kg (~112 mcg) sustains without overshoot.
150 mcg without load: slower, tends to exceed maintenance.
Pre-op target: fT4 normal by day 5–7, not TSH.
items.reference: Malhotra & Bhadada, IJEM 2022: "oral LT4 regimen consisting of a loading dose of 300–500 µg, followed by taper" when IV unavailable; ETJ 2021: "early biochemical recovery" with fT4 normalisation by day 4–5.

items.question: How should a resident manage this patient from now through surgery and early post-op?
items.answer: 1. Reduce LT4 to ~100 mcg/day now (≈1.6 mcg/kg). 2. Proceed with urgent oral-cancer surgery — do not delay for TSH equilibration. 3. Morning of surgery: give 100 mcg PO with sip of water. 4. Post-op NPO: may omit LT4 for 2–3 days if needed; resume 100 mcg PO when eating. 5. Recheck TSH + free T4 at 4–6 weeks post-op; titrate to TSH 0.5–2.5. 6. Retrospective learning: next similar case — morning cortisol before LT4, consider oral loading 300–500 mcg day 1 → ~112 mcg maintenance, target fT4 normal by day 5–7.
keyPoints: Reduce to ~100 mcg now; operate without TSH-delay.
Day-of-surgery PO dose; brief post-op omission safe.
TFT at 4–6 weeks post-op; titrate to TSH 0.5–2.5.
Next case: adrenal screen + oral loading → ~112 mcg maintenance.
items.reference: Case management synthesis: "reduce to 100 mcg"; Palace et al.: "administered on the day of surgery via the enteral route"; Malhotra & Bhadada: "loading dose of 300–500 µg" then maintenance.

items.title: How settled is this approach — strengths, limitations, and regional caveats
items.content: Strengths: the team correctly identified severe hypothyroidism, started prompt replacement, rechecked TFT at ~2 weeks, and achieved biochemical fitness for urgent onco surgery — the right outcome. Reducing to ~100 mcg now is well-supported by weight-based dosing (ATA 2014). Limitations and doubt-flags: the precise number (100 vs 112 mcg) is individualized — either is reasonable at ~1.6 mcg/kg; final dose is set by post-op TSH. Oral loading evidence is strongest in myxedema coma cohorts; extrapolation to 'severe but not comatose' pre-op patients is clinically logical but not from a large RCT. Adrenal screening evidence in TSH >100 is growing but cortisol thresholds and empiric hydrocortisone practice vary by centre and guideline. IV LT4 loading remains gold standard where available; oral loading is a pragmatic, increasingly supported alternative in resource-limited settings (India). Age 60 with possible occult CAD: if angina or arrhythmia develops on LT4, reduce dose and involve cardiology — 'urgent' does not mean ignore cardiac red flags.
keyPoints: Solid: prompt replacement, fT4-targeted fitness, reduce to ~1.6 mcg/kg now.
Flagged: 100 vs 112 mcg is minor; oral-loading evidence mainly from myxedema series.
Regional: IV unavailable → oral load pragmatic; adrenal screen thresholds vary.
Age 60: watch for cardiac red flags on LT4 even when urgency is high.
items.reference: Module appraisal: Malhotra & Bhadada IJEM 2022 — "oral LT4 regimen consisting of a loading dose of 300–500 µg"; ATA 2014 — "1.6 mcg/kg body weight per day"; case TSH "1.67" on 150 mcg.

items.assertion: An oral levothyroxine loading dose achieves faster surgical fitness in severe hypothyroidism.
items.reason: Because severe hypothyroidism depletes the total body T4 pool, requiring rapid replenishment to normalize fT4 within days.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Loading doses rapidly replenish depleted T4 stores, normalizing fT4 quickly, whereas gradual dosing without loading relies on slower pool repletion and may overshoot maintenance needs.
items.distillation_trace:

items.stem: A 55-year-old patient with newly diagnosed hypothyroidism requiring urgent oral cancer surgery is being managed by a resident. Which of the following is the most appropriate management strategy from now through surgery and early post-operative period?
options: A) Reduce LT4 to ~100 mcg/day immediately, proceed with surgery without delaying for TSH equilibration, administer 100 mcg PO on morning of surgery, omit LT4 for 2–3 days post-op if NPO, and recheck TSH + free T4 at 4–6 weeks post-op
B) Delay surgery until TSH is within normal range, administer 50 mcg LT4 on morning of surgery, continue LT4 during post-op NPO period, and recheck TSH at 2 weeks post-op
C) Maintain current LT4 dose, administer 100 mcg IV on morning of surgery, continue LT4 during post-op NPO period, and recheck TSH at 6 weeks post-op
D) Reduce LT4 to 50 mcg/day immediately, proceed with surgery, omit LT4 during post-op NPO period, and recheck TSH at 2 weeks post-op
items.explanation: Immediate reduction to ~100 mcg/day is recommended to avoid thyrotoxicosis while allowing surgical urgency. LT4 should be administered on morning of surgery with water, and brief post-op omission is acceptable. TSH and free T4 should be rechecked at 4–6 weeks post-op with target TSH 0.5–2.5 mIU/L.
